Term: SKU
Full Form: Stock Keeping Unit
Explanation: Unique ID for each product variant
Example: Red, Medium Nike T-shirt
Why Needed: Granular product tracking
Used By: Inventory, Merchandising

Term: UPC
Full Form: Universal Product Code
Explanation: 12-digit barcode for scanning
Example: Barcode for Lay's chips
Why Needed: Standardized identification
Used By: POS, IT, Master Data

Term: Assortment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Variety of products in a category
Example: 6 coffee brands × 3 sizes
Why Needed: Enhances choice & planning
Used By: Merchandising, Category Managers

Term: Stock Turnover
Full Form: --
Explanation: Frequency of inventory sale & replacement
Example: 4 shampoo restocks/year
Why Needed: Indicates movement
Used By: Inventory, Finance

Term: On-Hand Inventory
Full Form: --
Explanation: Currently available stock
Example: 18 Dove shampoos in store
Why Needed: Aids restocking
Used By: WMS, Store Ops

Term: Safety Stock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Buffer stock for demand spikes
Example: 10 extra iPhones held
Why Needed: Avoids stockouts
Used By: Planning, Supply Chain

Term: Reorder Point
Full Form: --
Explanation: Level triggering auto reordering
Example: <20 toilet rolls = auto order
Why Needed: Ensures availability
Used By: Inventory System

Term: Cycle Count
Full Form: --
Explanation: Periodic partial stock count
Example: Weekly aisle count
Why Needed: Keeps records accurate
Used By: Store Ops, Inventory Control

Term: Dead Stock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Unsellable or obsolete stock
Example: Halloween candy in Dec
Why Needed: Requires clearance
Used By: Merchandising, Finance

Term: Inventory Turn
Full Form: --
Explanation: Annual inventory turnover rate
Example: 8 turns/year
Why Needed: Measures efficiency
Used By: Finance, Merchandising

Term: Fill Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of demand met from inventory
Example: 95 fulfilled out of 100
Why Needed: Indicates service level
Used By: Supply Chain, Planning

Term: Stock-to-Sales Ratio
Full Form: --
Explanation: Stock level vs. sales volume
Example: ₹100K stock/₹20K sales
Why Needed: Identifies overstocking
Used By: Finance, Merchandising

Term: MOQ
Full Form: Minimum Order Quantity
Explanation: Smallest orderable quantity
Example: MOQ = 100 shirts
Why Needed: Ensures supplier efficiency
Used By: Procurement

Term: Backorder
Full Form: --
Explanation: Demand for out-of-stock items
Example: Console on backorder
Why Needed: Retains demand
Used By: Order Mgmt, Inventory

Term: Auto Replenishment
Full Form: --
Explanation: System-driven stock reorder
Example: Inventory < 30 → auto order
Why Needed: Saves manual work
Used By: Planning, IT

Term: Demand Forecasting
Full Form: --
Explanation: Predicts future sales using data
Example: 10K jackets for winter
Why Needed: Enables accurate planning
Used By: Planners, Buyers

Term: ATP
Full Form: Available to Promise
Explanation: Uncommitted available inventory
Example: 50 units ATP = 50 units shippable
Why Needed: Avoids false promises
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Assortment Rationalization
Full Form: --
Explanation: Streamlining SKUs to optimize
Example: Removing slow sellers
Why Needed: Improves profit/mix
Used By: Merchandising

Term: Expiry Management
Full Form: --
Explanation: Tracking products with expiry dates
Example: Dairy products with 7-day shelf life
Why Needed: Prevents waste
Used By: Inventory, WMS

Term: Overstock
Full Form: --
Explanation: Inventory beyond forecasted demand
Example: 1000 units instead of 600
Why Needed: Ties up capital
Used By: Inventory Planners

Term: POS
Full Form: Point of Sale
Explanation: Checkout system for transactions
Example: Scanning at cash counter
Why Needed: Updates billing & stock
Used By: Store Ops, IT

Term: Planogram
Full Form: --
Explanation: Shelf arrangement layout
Example: Toothpaste planogram
Why Needed: Improves display consistency
Used By: Visual Merchandising

Term: Shrinkage
Full Form: --
Explanation: Inventory loss from theft/errors
Example: 5 items missing
Why Needed: Impacts margins
Used By: Loss Prevention

Term: Facing
Full Form: --
Explanation: No. of products visible on shelf
Example: 4 Coke bottles front-facing
Why Needed: Attracts customer attention
Used By: Merchandising

Term: BOH / FOH
Full Form: Back / Front of House
Explanation: Stockroom vs. shop floor
Example: Stock BOH, sales FOH
Why Needed: Helps organization
Used By: Ops, Store Staff

Term: Stock Replenishment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Refilling shelves from stock
Example: Milk restocked on aisle
Why Needed: Maintains availability
Used By: Store Team

Term: Stock Cover
Full Form: --
Explanation: How long stock will last
Example: 4 weeks of shampoo
Why Needed: Guides purchase
Used By: Planning, Merchandising

Term: Mystery Shopping
Full Form: --
Explanation: Evaluation done via disguised customers
Example: Review of store service by hired shopper
Why Needed: Quality control & compliance
Used By: CX, Retail Operations

Term: Plan Compliance
Full Form: --
Explanation: Adherence to store setup plans
Example: Store A follows display plan 100%
Why Needed: Standardization across stores
Used By: Store Audit Team

Term: GRN
Full Form: Goods Receipt Note
Explanation: Confirms goods received
Example: 50 jeans GRN generated
Why Needed: Triggers payment & update
Used By: Warehouse, Finance

Term: ASN
Full Form: Advanced Shipping Notice
Explanation: Pre-arrival shipping info
Example: 200 laptops ASN sent
Why Needed: Preps receiving team
Used By: Vendor Mgmt, WH

Term: Putaway
Full Form: --
Explanation: Placing goods in storage
Example: Soaps to Bin A7
Why Needed: Space utilization
Used By: Warehouse Ops

Term: Picking
Full Form: --
Explanation: Selecting items to fulfill orders
Example: Shirt & jeans picked
Why Needed: Affects accuracy & speed
Used By: Fulfillment Team

Term: Cross-Docking
Full Form: --
Explanation: No storage; direct ship from inbound to outbound
Example: Shoes received and shipped
Why Needed: Cuts storage time
Used By: DC, Logistics

Term: Lead Time
Full Form: --
Explanation: Time between order & delivery
Example: 10 days for cereal
Why Needed: Affects planning
Used By: Procurement, Planning

Term: Slotting
Full Form: --
Explanation: Strategic item placement
Example: Milk near dispatch door
Why Needed: Enhances pick speed
Used By: WMS Admins

Term: Reverse Logistics
Full Form: --
Explanation: Handling returns
Example: Blender returned & sent to vendor
Why Needed: Customer satisfaction
Used By: Returns, Customer Care

Term: ATP
Full Form: Available to Promise
Explanation: Unallocated stock
Example: 100 ATP units = can sell 100
Why Needed: Real-time order commitment
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Pick-to-Light / Put-to-Light
Full Form: --
Explanation: Lights guide picking/putaway
Example: Light flashes at Bin B3
Why Needed: Reduces picking errors
Used By: Warehouse Ops

Term: DC
Full Form: Distribution Center
Explanation: Central storage/dispatch hub
Example: Orders ship from DC
Why Needed: Fast fulfillment
Used By: Logistics

Term: Omnichannel Order Routing
Full Form: --
Explanation: Assign order to optimal node
Example: Route order to nearest store
Why Needed: Reduces cost, speeds up
Used By: Order Mgmt

Term: Cross-Border Fulfillment
Full Form: --
Explanation: Shipping items to another country
Example: Shipping apparel from US to Canada
Why Needed: Expands market
Used By: Logistics, Compliance

Term: Bulk Picking
Full Form: --
Explanation: Picking multiple orders simultaneously
Example: 5 shirts picked for 3 orders
Why Needed: Improves efficiency
Used By: Warehouse Planning

Term: Markdown
Full Form: --
Explanation: Price cut for promotions or clearance
Example: ₹999 → ₹599
Why Needed: Clears inventory
Used By: Merchandising

Term: Sell-Through
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of stock sold in period
Example: 80% sold in 2 weeks
Why Needed: Assesses item performance
Used By: Buyers

Term: Lift
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sales boost due to promotion
Example: Chips sell more near soda
Why Needed: Marketing impact
Used By: Trade Team

Term: Basket Size
Full Form: --
Explanation: Avg. items or ₹ per transaction
Example: 5 items/customer
Why Needed: Shows engagement
Used By: Store Ops

Term: GMROI
Full Form: Gross Margin Return on Investment
Explanation: Profit per ₹1 of inventory
Example: ₹200 profit on ₹100 = 2.0 GMROI
Why Needed: Evaluates SKU efficiency
Used By: Merch, Finance

Term: Visual Merchandising
Full Form: --
Explanation: Displaying products to boost sales
Example: Wedding suits in front window
Why Needed: Attracts footfall
Used By: Visual Team

Term: Plan vs Actual
Full Form: --
Explanation: Planned vs. real performance
Example: Target ₹5L, achieved ₹4.8L
Why Needed: Course correction
Used By: Sales, Analytics

Term: Promotion Calendar
Full Form: --
Explanation: Scheduled promotional plan
Example: Diwali Sale in October
Why Needed: Drives campaigns
Used By: Marketing, Merchandising

Term: Conversion Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: Visitors who purchase
Example: 100 visitors, 20 buyers = 20%
Why Needed: Measures effectiveness
Used By: eCom, Store Sales

Term: Omnichannel
Full Form: --
Explanation: Unified experience across all channels
Example: Browse app → buy store
Why Needed: Customer convenience
Used By: CX, eCom

Term: BOPIS
Full Form: Buy Online Pickup In-Store
Explanation: Online order → store pickup
Example: Charger pickup from MW store
Why Needed: Saves delivery time/cost
Used By: Ops, eCom

Term: ROPIS
Full Form: Reserve Online Pickup In-Store
Explanation: Online reserve → pay/pick later
Example: Dress reserved → tried in-store
Why Needed: Brings foot traffic
Used By: Store Ops

Term: Click & Collect
Full Form: --
Explanation: Same as BOPIS (Europe term)
Example: Grocery ordered online, collected
Why Needed: Convenience
Used By: Store + eCom

Term: Dropshipping
Full Form: --
Explanation: Vendor ships directly to customer
Example: Sunglasses from third party
Why Needed: Saves inventory cost
Used By: eCom, Vendor Mgmt

Term: Last Mile Delivery
Full Form: --
Explanation: Final delivery leg to customer
Example: Order sent by courier
Why Needed: Crucial for satisfaction
Used By: Logistics

Term: Endless Aisle
Full Form: --
Explanation: In-store access to online inventory
Example: Customer orders color not in-store
Why Needed: Increases conversion
Used By: Omnichannel, eCom

Term: Ship-from-Store
Full Form: --
Explanation: Orders shipped directly from stores
Example: Fulfilling online order from nearby store
Why Needed: Speeds delivery
Used By: Order Mgmt, Store Ops

Term: CRM
Full Form: Customer Relationship Mgmt
Explanation: Centralized customer data & comms
Example: Stores purchase history
Why Needed: Personalized offers
Used By: Marketing, IT

Term: CLV
Full Form: Customer Lifetime Value
Explanation: Total value of a customer
Example: ₹10K/year × 5 = ₹50K
Why Needed: Segments loyal customers
Used By: Analytics

Term: Churn Rate
Full Form: --
Explanation: % of customers lost over time
Example: 20% churn in loyalty program
Why Needed: Retention metric
Used By: Loyalty Team

Term: NPS
Full Form: Net Promoter Score
Explanation: Satisfaction measure (0-10 scale)
Example: Rated 9 → Promoter
Why Needed: Brand health indicator
Used By: CX Team

Term: Loyalty Program
Full Form: --
Explanation: Points/benefits for repeat purchases
Example: ₹1 = 1 pt; 1000 pts = ₹500
Why Needed: Builds loyalty
Used By: CRM, Marketing

Term: Segmentation
Full Form: --
Explanation: Grouping customers by behavior/value
Example: Budget vs. premium shoppers
Why Needed: Tailors marketing
Used By: Analytics, CRM

Term: Gross Margin
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sale price - COGS
Example: ₹500 - ₹300 = ₹200
Why Needed: Tracks profit
Used By: Finance

Term: Sell-In vs Sell-Out
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sell-In: to retailer; Sell-Out: to customer
Example: 100 shirts sold to store; 80 sold to customers
Why Needed: Tracks sell-through
Used By: Merchandising

Term: OTB
Full Form: Open to Buy
Explanation: Budget left for purchasing stock
Example: ₹5L left this quarter
Why Needed: Controls buying
Used By: Planning, Finance

Term: Cost-to-Serve
Full Form: --
Explanation: All costs to fulfill an order
Example: ₹300 to deliver to Ladakh
Why Needed: Price strategy input
Used By: Strategy, Finance

Term: Forecast Accuracy
Full Form: --
Explanation: How close forecasts match sales
Example: 90% accurate prediction
Why Needed: Helps avoid over/understock
Used By: Analytics

Term: Sales per Sq. Ft.
Full Form: --
Explanation: Sales from 1 sq. ft. of store space
Example: ₹8000/sq. ft.
Why Needed: Measures store performance
Used By: Retail Ops

Term: Markdown Optimization
Full Form: --
Explanation: Strategy to reduce price profitably
Example: ₹2,000 → ₹1,500 to boost sales
Why Needed: Preserves margin
Used By: Pricing, Analytics

Term: Inventory Valuation
Full Form: --
Explanation: Assigning financial value to stock
Example: ₹5L stock at year-end
Why Needed: Needed for accounting
Used By: Finance

Term: Breakeven Point
Full Form: --
Explanation: Revenue needed to cover all costs
Example: ₹1L fixed costs, ₹2K margin/product = 50 units
Why Needed: Determines profit threshold
Used By: Finance, Strategy

We'll be covering five core process flows that are key to understanding how retail works:

It includes deciding what to buy, choosing vendors, placing purchase orders, receiving goods, and checking quality. Good procurement controls cost and keeps supply steady. 
Let's discuss how this process flow goes step by step

text:
Slide 2: Key Actors and Systems Involved
Narrator: "Here are the key actors and systems involved in procurement."

Actors:
  - Procurement Initiator/User Team: Identifies the need and initiates the request.
  - Procurement/Purchase Team: Handles sourcing, vendor evaluation, PO creation, and approvals.
  - Supplier: Provides the goods or services.
  - Warehouse Team: Verifies and accepts goods.
  - Finance Team: Approves payments and processes transactions.

Systems:
  - ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): Handles PO, GRN, inventory, and finance.
  - Document Management System (DMS): Stores POs, GRNs, invoices.
  - Communication Lane: Sends alerts and approvals.

It covers planning, selecting vendors, negotiation, receiving goods, and payment."

text: Slide 1: Introduction to Inventory Management - The Inventory Management Process Flow is about keeping track of products. It includes receiving goods, storing them safely, checking stock levels, and restocking when needed. Good inventory management prevents running out of items or having too much stock.

text:
Slide 2: Key Actors and Systems Involved
  - Narrator:
"Let's meet the key actors and systems that bring this process to life."

Actors:
  - Warehouse Team: Receives goods and verifies them.
  - Inventory Control Team: Handles stock audits, discrepancy checks, and inventory updates.
  - Ecommerce/Marketplace Team: Manages live listings on platforms like Amazon or Flipkart.
  - Communication Lane: Alerts and notifications to internal/external stakeholders.
  - Document Management System (DMS): Stores scanned documents and records like GRNs.

Systems:
  - WMS (Warehouse Management System): Tracks goods inside the warehouse.
  - ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): Core system handling inventory, finance, and procurement.
  - OMS (Order Management System): Syncs inventory with marketplaces and sales channels.

text:
Slide 3: What is Inventory Management?
  - 🎙️ Narrator:
"Inventory Management is the backbone of retail.
It ensures that stock is recorded, allocated, verified, and available to customers when and where they want it -- without understocking or overstocking."

Slide 12: Summary and Terminology Recap
  - Let's recap the key terminologies covered in this flow:
  - POS (Point of Sale): System used for billing and checkout in-store.
  - OMS (Order Management System): Centralized system managing order placement, sourcing, and fulfillment.
  - Endless Aisle: Ordering model that allows customers to buy unavailable items from other stores or warehouses.
  - ATP (Available to Promise): Real-time stock available to fulfill new orders.
  - OOS (Out of Stock): Condition when no stock is available across locations.
  - DMS (Document Management System): Logs and manages all order, payment, and inventory documents.

text:
Slide 2: Actors and Systems Involved
  - Narrator:
Let's begin with the key participants in this process.

Actors:
  - Customer - who initiates the return.
  - Store Staff - responsible for scanning and accepting in-store returns.
  - Warehouse/Quality Check Executive - who checks returned product condition.
  - Finance Team - who handles refunds.

Systems:
  - Return Management System (RMS) - captures return requests and manages eligibility.
  - Order Management System (OMS) - logs and processes return orders.
  - POS (Point of Sale) - used for in-store return scanning and receipts.
  - Payment Gateway - handles refunds.
  - DMS (Document Management System) - stores return documents like Return ID, refund status, and transaction proof.
  - Communication Lane - sends alerts to customers at different stages.

text:
Slide 3: Customer Initiates Return
  - Narrator:
The process starts when a customer initiates a return -- this could be via the app, website, store, or even by calling customer support.
  - The Return Management System captures key return details:
  - Order ID
  - Reason for return
  - Channel of initiation
  - RMS (Return Management System): Platform used to handle returns, eligibility checks, and return types.

metadata.process_name:

text:
Slide 4: Validation of Return Eligibility
  - Narrator:
Next, the system checks whether the return request is within the valid return window -- for example, 7 or 10 days from delivery.
If the return window has expired, the request is rejected, and the customer is notified.
  - If within the window, the system checks if the item is return-eligible based on:
  - Condition
  - Tags
  - Product category
  - If not eligible, rejection reason is shared.
  - Return Window: The number of days post-purchase within which a return is allowed.

If the return is valid, the customer chooses a return method:
  - In-store drop or
  - Courier pickup
  - In either case, a Return Order is created in the OMS with a unique Return ID.
  - Return ID: A unique number generated to track the return journey.

Slide 8: Quality Check (QC)
  - Narrator:
Once received, the warehouse or QC team performs a quality check on the item.
They verify if the product meets return conditions -- no damage, original tags intact, etc.

If it fails QC, refund is rejected, and the customer is informed.
  - QC (Quality Check): Inspection process to assess item condition before refund approval.

It covers verifying eligibility, checking item condition, issuing refunds or replacements, and updating inventory. Good returns handling builds trust and gives insights to reduce future issues. 

Let's discuss how this process flow goes step by step.

text:
Slide 9: Refund Processing
  - Narrator:
If approved, the finance team processes the refund:
  - Refund is either issued to the original payment mode or
  - Provided as store credit or wallet balance, as applicable.
  - The transaction flows through the payment gateway.
  - Payment Gateway: A secure platform that processes digital payments and refunds.

Slide 10: Update Inventory & Order Status
  - Narrator:
Once refund is completed:
  - OMS updates the order status to "Returned"
  - Inventory is adjusted accordingly
  - A notification is sent to the customer confirming refund completion with an expected timeline
  - All data is saved in the DMS for future reference.
  - DMS (Document Management System): Stores digital records such as Return IDs, refund statuses, and tracking data.

text:
Slide 12: Terminologies Recap
  - Narrator:
Here's a quick recap of key terms used in this process:
  - RMS: Return Management System - handles eligibility checks and return creation.
  - OMS: Order Management System - tracks order and return statuses.
  - POS: Point of Sale system - scans returns in-store.
  - Return ID: Unique identifier for each return request.
  - QC: Quality Check - ensures item condition meets return criteria.
  - Payment Gateway: Facilitates digital refund transactions.
  - DMS: Document Management System - saves logs like Return ID, refund status, and proof.
  - Final Slide: Thank You
  - Narrator:
Thank you for watching this video on the Return & Logistics Flow.
This step-by-step process ensures a smooth experience for customers while keeping backend systems and operations aligned.

text: Retail 101 Training

Slide 1 - An Introduction to the Retail Sector
  - Slide Content (shown on PPT):
  - Retail Ecosystem & Value Chain - from manufacturers to customers
  - Retail Formats - supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce, omnichannel
  - Retail Processes - procurement, inventory, fulfillment, customer service
  - Retail Systems - POS, ERP, OMS & supporting technologies
  - Regulations & KPIs - compliance & performance measures
  - Emerging Trends - digital tech, data-driven practices, evolving expectations
  - Narration Script (to be spoken):
"Welcome to this Retail 101 program. This session will give you a solid foundation in how the retail sector works.
  - We'll start by understanding the retail ecosystem and value chain - how goods and value flow from manufacturers all the way to customers.
  - Next, we'll look at different retail formats, including supermarkets, convenience stores, e-commerce platforms, and omnichannel models that combine online and offline experiences.
  - We'll then dive into the core retail processes like procurement, inventory management, order fulfillment, and customer service - all of which ensure smooth operations.
  - Supporting these processes are critical retail systems such as POS, ERP, and OMS that help retailers run efficiently.
  - We'll also touch upon regulations and KPIs - covering compliance requirements and how performance is measured.
  - Finally, we'll explore emerging trends, such as the use of digital technologies, data-driven practices, and how retailers are adapting to changing customer expectations.
  - This introduction sets the stage for the deeper dive into each area in the upcoming slides."

metadata.slide_title: An Introduction to the Retail Sector

text:
Slide 2 - Agenda - Retail 101 Training
Slide Content (shown on PPT):
  - Module 1: Introduction to Retail
  - Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
  - Module 3: Core Retail Concepts
  - Module 4: Retail Operations
  - Module 5: Governance & Performance
  - Module 6: Retail Landscape & Future
  - Module 7: Wrap-up & Knowledge Check

Narration
  - "Here's the agenda for our Retail 101 training.
We'll begin with Module 1, where we introduce the retail ecosystem, its importance, the evolution of retail, and the different retail formats.
Module 2 will familiarize you with the most common retail terminologies and acronyms like POS, SKU, and ERP, helping you understand how they're used in daily operations.
In Module 3, we'll dive into core retail concepts, including the retail value chain, customer journey, and assortment management.
Module 4 is about retail operations - we'll cover supply chain basics, key retail systems like POS, OMS, WMS, ERP, and CRM, and also look at omnichannel retail, payments, private labels, and loss prevention.
Module 5 focuses on governance and performance, where we'll discuss compliance requirements and the KPIs used to measure retail success.
Module 6 will take a broader view of the retail landscape - looking at global and Indian leaders, the role of technology, and emerging trends shaping the future of retail.
Finally, in Module 7, we'll wrap up the program with a summary of key learnings, and conduct a short knowledge check or Q&A to reinforce your understanding."

Slide 3 - PPT Content (on slide)
  - Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail
This module covers:
  - Retail Ecosystem - Overview
  - Importance of Retail
  - Evolution of Retail
  - Types of Retail Formats

Narration
  - "Welcome to Module 1: Introduction to Retail.
This module is designed to give you the foundation you need before we move into detailed retail processes.
  - We'll be covering four key areas: the retail ecosystem, the importance of retail, its evolution over time, and different retail formats.
  - Before we dive deeper, let's start with the Retail Ecosystem. Think of it as the big picture -- all the players and functions working together to deliver a product or service to the end consumer.
  - In the next slide, we'll explore how this ecosystem flows step by step, from the manufacturer all the way to the customer."

Slide 4 - PPT Content (on slide) - Retail Ecosystem: Overview - Retail = selling goods/services directly to consumers (final stage of supply chain). - Flow: Manufacturer → Wholesaler/Distributor → Retailer → Customer. - Key Enablers: Logistics | Finance | IT (POS/ERP) | Marketing. - Retail is customer-facing - trust, convenience, and brand perception depend on it. - Example: Nike - manufactured in Vietnam → shipped to warehouses → sold via Nike stores/online → customer purchase defines the retail moment. - Narration (to be spoken) - "Retail is the process of selling goods and services directly to end consumers. It represents the final stage of the supply chain -- where products finally reach the people who use them. - The flow is simple: manufacturers create products, which then move to wholesalers or distributors, then to retailers, and finally to customers. - Supporting this flow are key enablers such as logistics for storage and transport, finance for payments and credit, IT systems like POS and ERP for smooth operations, and marketing to drive demand. - What makes retail unique is that it is customer-facing. Retailers are not just moving goods; they are building trust and convenience at the last mile. A poor retail experience can damage the perception of the entire brand. - Take Nike, for example. Shoes may be manufactured in Vietnam, shipped to Nike warehouses, and then sold through Nike's own stores or online platforms. The customer's final buying experience -- whether in-store or online -- is what defines retail. - In essence, retail is the bridge between the world of production and the world of consumption." Slide 5 - Importance of Retail - Key Points: - Economic Growth - Major GDP contributor (India: ~10%, US: ~20%). - Employment - 2nd largest employer in India after agriculture. - Customer Value - Offers choice, convenience, and competitive pricing. - Innovation Hub - Drives change (e.g., Amazon Go, Zepto's 10-min delivery). - Narration "Retail is much more than just selling products. It's a powerful engine of the economy, contributing around 10% of India's GDP and nearly 20% in the US. It also creates millions of jobs, making it the second-largest employer in India after agriculture. For customers, retail ensures choice, convenience, and competitive pricing across multiple formats - whether offline, online, or through quick commerce. And beyond that, retail is constantly innovating. New models like Amazon Go's cashier-less stores or Zepto's 10-minute delivery are redefining consumer expectations every day." Slide 6 - Evolution of Retail (1/3): Traditional Retail - PPT Content: - Dominated by kirana & mom-and-pop stores. - Built on trust, relationships, and community. - Customers often purchased on credit. - Personalized shopping experience. - Focus on convenience & reliability, not discounts. - Example: Local kirana delivering groceries & maintaining handwritten dues. - Narration: "In the early stages, retail in India and across the world was dominated by small kirana shops and mom-and-pop stores. These stores were deeply community-driven, built on trust and personal relationships. Customers often purchased items on

not discounts. - Example: Local kirana delivering groceries & maintaining handwritten dues. - Narration: "In the early stages, retail in India and across the world was dominated by small kirana shops and mom-and-pop stores. These stores were deeply community-driven, built on trust and personal relationships. Customers often purchased items on credit, with payments settled later. The experience was highly personalized, as shopkeepers usually knew the family's preferences. The focus was not variety or discounts, but rather convenience and reliability. A good example is your neighborhood kirana store delivering groceries at home and keeping a handwritten record of dues."

Slide 7 - Evolution of Retail (2/3): Organized Retail
  - PPT Content:
  - Emerged with urbanization & economic growth.
  - Department Stores (1800s): Multiple categories, destination shopping.
  - Supermarkets/Hypermarkets (1900s): Self-service, bulk buying, discount pricing.
  - Discount Stores: Low prices, high volumes, value-conscious customers.
  - Examples: Walmart (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).
  - Narration:
"With urbanization and economic development, retail formats became more structured and organized. Department stores in the 1800s offered multiple categories under one roof, making them a one-stop destination. In the 1900s, supermarkets and hypermarkets introduced self-service, bulk buying, and discount pricing, turning shopping into an experience. Discount stores also emerged, focusing on affordability and attracting value-conscious shoppers. For example, Walmart became a global leader in supply chain efficiency, while in India, Big Bazaar popularized hypermarkets and DMart built its success on everyday low pricing."

Slide 8 - Evolution of Retail (3/3): Digital & Future Retail
  - PPT Content:
  - Technology shifted retail to digital-first.
  - E-commerce (2000s): Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra - shopping anytime, anywhere.
  - Omnichannel (2010s): Integrated online & offline journey.
  - Future Retail: AI personalization, AR/VR shopping, robotics, data-driven insights.
  - Example: AR try-on features, AI chatbots, Amazon robotics.
  - Narration:
"The next big transformation came with technology, moving retail from physical-first to digital-first. In the 2000s, e-commerce platforms like Amazon, Flipkart, and Myntra allowed customers to shop anytime, anywhere, breaking geographical barriers. The 2010s brought omnichannel retail, integrating offline and online journeys -- for example, browsing online but purchasing in-store or choosing click-and-collect options. Looking ahead, future retail will be shaped by AI-driven personalization, AR and VR shopping experiences, robotics in warehouses, and advanced data analytics. Examples include AR try-on features in fashion apps, AI chatbots for personalized recommendations, and Amazon's robotic warehouses."

metadata.slide_title: Evolution of Retail (3/3): Digital & Future Retail

text:
Slide 9 - Types of Retail Formats (1/2)
  - PPT Content:
  - Department Stores: Multiple categories under one roof.
  - Supermarkets: Groceries & essentials, self-service, competitive pricing.
  - Hypermarkets: Larger scale, groceries + clothing + electronics, bulk sales & discounts.
  - Examples: Shoppers Stop (India), Walmart (US), Carrefour (global), Big Bazaar & DMart (India).
  - Narration:
"Now let's look at the different types of retail formats. Department stores offer multiple categories under one roof -- from clothing and cosmetics to footwear and home furnishings. A good example is Shoppers Stop in India. Supermarkets focus mainly on groceries and daily essentials, usually in a self-service format with attractive pricing -- think Walmart in the US or Big Bazaar in India. Hypermarkets take this a step further, operating on a much larger scale. They combine groceries, clothing, electronics, and even appliances, with an emphasis on bulk sales and big discounts. Examples include Carrefour globally and DMart in India."

Slide 10 - Types of Retail Formats (2/2)
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Discount Stores → Low prices, high volumes (e.g., Dollar Tree, DMart)
  - Specialty Stores → Focus on single category, expertise & variety (e.g., Nike, Sephora)
  - Convenience Stores → Small format, quick buys near homes/offices (e.g., 7-Eleven)
  - E-commerce → Online shopping, price comparison, home delivery (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart)
  - Omnichannel → Seamless online + offline journey (e.g., Target, Reliance Digital)
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Moving forward, let's explore more formats.
  - We begin with Discount Stores, which are built around the idea of low prices and high volumes, making them highly attractive to price-sensitive customers. Think of Dollar Tree in the US or DMart in India.
  - Next are Specialty Stores. These focus on a single product category and build deep expertise, offering wide variety and enhanced customer experiences. Examples include Nike for sportswear or Sephora for beauty.
  - Then we have Convenience Stores. These are small outlets designed for quick purchases, often strategically located near homes or workplaces. 7-Eleven is a global leader in this space.
  - Of course, E-commerce has revolutionized retail. Online platforms like Amazon and Flipkart make it easy to shop from anywhere, compare prices, and get doorstep delivery.
  - And finally, we have Omnichannel Retail, which is the latest trend. It combines offline and online seamlessly - so customers can check availability online, try in-store, and even get home delivery. Retailers like Target in the US and Reliance Digital in India are actively adopting this model."

Slide 11 - 
Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Retail has its own vocabulary
  - Used by managers, buyers, supply chain, and tech teams
  - Learn common acronyms & terms: POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, etc.
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Welcome to Module 2: Key Retail Terminologies. Just like every profession has its own language, retail too has a vocabulary that's widely used by store managers, buyers, supply chain teams, and even technology partners. In this module, we'll get familiar with the most important retail terms and acronyms--like POS, SKU, ERP, CRM, and many more--that you'll come across in day-to-day retail operations."

Slide 12 - Store & Product Related Terms
  - Content (for PPT):
  - SKU (Stock Keeping Unit): Unique ID per product variant
  - UPC / Barcode: Standardized code for scanning & billing
  - Category Management: Grouping products to improve sales
  - Planogram (POG): Guide for store product placement
  - Private Label: Retailer-owned brands
  - Assortment: Range of products offered
  - Facing: Number of product units visible on shelf
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Let's begin with some store and product-related terms.
First, SKU or Stock Keeping Unit - this is a unique ID for each product variant, like a blue shirt in size M.
Next, UPC or Barcode - this is the standardized code you see on products, used for scanning and billing at the checkout.
Then, Category Management - the practice of logically grouping products to optimize sales and make shopping easier for customers.
We also have the Planogram, or POG, which is essentially a store layout guide showing exactly where products should be placed on shelves.
Another key concept is Private Label, which refers to retailer-owned brands--for example, Amazon Basics or DMart Ready.
Assortment refers to the range of products that a store or online platform offers.
And finally, Facing is the number of product units visible to customers on the shelf--it's about presentation and visibility."

Slide 13 - Sales & Checkout Terms
  - Content (for PPT) →
  - POS (Point of Sale): Billing system (cash registers, digital POS).
  - Basket Size: Average number of items per transaction.
  - Footfall: Number of customers visiting a store.
  - Conversion Rate: % of visitors who actually purchase.
  - Shrinkage: Inventory loss due to theft, damage, or errors.
  - AOV (Average Order Value): Average spend per customer order.
  - Cross-selling / Upselling: Suggesting additional or premium products at checkout.
  - Narration (for delivery) 

Now, let's move into some sales and checkout terms that retailers use every day:
  - POS, or Point of Sale, refers to the system used for billing--this could be a traditional cash register, or a digital POS that supports card payments, UPI, or even mobile wallets.
  - Basket Size shows how many items, on average, a customer buys in a single transaction. A higher basket size usually means better sales per visit.
  - Footfall is simply the count of people walking into a store. But what really matters is how many of them actually buy something--this is tracked by the Conversion Rate.
  - Shrinkage is a challenge for retailers--it means loss of inventory because of theft, damage, or clerical errors.
  - AOV, or Average Order Value, tracks the average amount spent per transaction. For example, if a store has 100 orders worth $5,000 in total, the AOV is $50.
  - Finally, cross-selling and upselling are sales techniques at checkout. Cross-selling is about recommending a related product--like offering fries with a burger--while upselling is about suggesting a premium option, like a bigger size or higher-end model.

Slide 14 - Supply Chain & Inventory Terms
  - Content (for PPT):
  - ERP: Integrated system for operations.
  - RFQ: Requesting supplier price quotes.
  - PO: Official order sent to supplier.
  - GRN: Record of goods received.
  - WMS: Manages warehouse storage & movement.
  - OMS: Manages customer orders across channels.
  - FIFO: Oldest stock sold first.
  - Lead Time: Time from order to receipt.
  - Narration (for delivery):
"In retail, supply chain and inventory terms are crucial for smooth operations.
ERP, or Enterprise Resource Planning, is the integrated backbone system that connects procurement, finance, inventory, and sales.
RFQ is a request for suppliers to share their price quotes, while a PO or Purchase Order is the formal document that confirms the purchase.
When goods arrive, they are documented through a GRN, or Goods Receipt Note.
Inside the warehouse, a WMS, or Warehouse Management System, keeps track of how items are stored, moved, and dispatched.
The OMS, or Order Management System, handles customer orders across online and offline channels.
FIFO, First In First Out, ensures older stock is sold before new stock, reducing waste.
And finally, Lead Time measures the time taken between placing an order and actually receiving it -- a key factor for planning stock levels."

Slide 15 - Marketing & Customer Terms
  - Content (for PPT):
  - CRM: Tracks customer interactions.
  - Loyalty Programs: Rewards for retention.
  - Merchandising: Displays, offers, pricing.
  - Promotion Calendar: Scheduled discounts & campaigns.
  - CLV: Total long-term customer value.
  - Market Basket Analysis: Products often bought together.
  - Churn Rate: % of customers lost.
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Retail success heavily depends on customer-focused strategies.
CRM, or Customer Relationship Management, helps retailers track interactions and history with each customer.
Loyalty programs such as reward points or cashback encourage customers to keep coming back.
Merchandising focuses on how products are displayed, priced, and promoted inside the store.
A promotion calendar is a planned schedule of discounts, festive offers, and campaigns.
Customer Lifetime Value, or CLV, estimates how much revenue a single customer contributes over their entire relationship with the retailer.
Market Basket Analysis helps identify products that are frequently purchased together -- for example, chips and soft drinks.
And lastly, Churn Rate shows the percentage of customers who stop buying, helping retailers know when they need to improve retention efforts."

Slide 16 - Omnichannel & Digital Terms
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Omnichannel: Seamless online + offline shopping.
  - Click & Collect / BOPIS: Order online, pick up in-store.
  - Dropshipping: Supplier ships directly to customer.
  - Last Mile Delivery: Final delivery to customer's doorstep.
  - Endless Aisle: Order out-of-stock items via kiosk/app.
  - Dark Store: Store used only for fulfilling online orders.
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Today's retail is driven by digital and omnichannel strategies.
Omnichannel retailing means giving customers a seamless shopping experience across both online and offline platforms.
Click and Collect, or BOPIS, allows customers to order online and pick up items at a nearby store.
Dropshipping is a model where the retailer sells products, but the supplier ships them directly to the customer.
Last Mile Delivery refers to the critical final step of getting the product to the customer's doorstep.
Endless Aisle enables customers to order items that aren't physically available in-store, usually via kiosks or mobile apps.
And a Dark Store is essentially a retail space dedicated only to fulfilling online orders, with no walk-in customers.
These digital innovations are shaping the future of retail."

Slide 17
Module 3 - Introduction to Retail Value Chain
  - Content (for PPT)
  - Covers the complete journey: Supplier → Customer.
  - Includes product, money, and information flow.
  - Key Stages:
  - Procurement
  - Inbound Logistics
  - Inventory Management
  - Merchandising
  - Sales
  - Fulfillment
  - Customer Service
  - Narration (for delivery)
"Welcome to Module 3: Retail Value Chain. In this module, we'll explore the full journey of retail - how a product moves from the supplier to the customer, and how money and information flow alongside. The value chain is made up of seven key stages: Procurement, Inbound Logistics, Inventory Management, Merchandising, Sales, Fulfillment, and finally, Customer Service. Understanding this chain helps us see the bigger picture of how retail businesses operate end-to-end."

Slide 18 - Procurement in Retail
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Backbone of retail: sourcing & purchasing products
  - Step 1: Requirement Identification
  - Step 2: Request for Quotation (RFQ)
  - Step 3: Supplier Selection
  - Step 4: Purchase Order (PO)
  - Step 5: Goods Receipt Note (GRN)
  - Step 6: Payment Processing
  - Ensures right product, price & timing
  - Narration (for delivery):
Procurement is the backbone of retail because it decides what products reach the customer and when. Let's break this into steps. First, retailers identify requirements - what product is needed, how much, and by when. For example, a store may need 10,000 shirts before the festive season.
  - Next, they raise an RFQ, or Request for Quotation, to compare prices, delivery timelines, and quality from different suppliers. Based on this, the retailer selects the supplier offering the best value - balancing cost, quality, and reliability.
  - Once chosen, a Purchase Order, or PO, is issued. This is a legal contract confirming details like product, price, and delivery dates. When goods arrive, a Goods Receipt Note (GRN) is prepared after checking quantity and quality. Finally, the invoice is verified, and payment is processed.
  - In short, procurement ensures retailers get the right product, at the right price, at the right time - a foundation for smooth inventory management and customer satisfaction.

metadata.slide_title: Module 3 - Introduction to Retail Value Chain

text:
Slide 19 - Inbound Logistics
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Movement of goods: Supplier → DC → Stores
  - Steps:
  - Supplier Dispatch
  - Transportation Mode (road/rail/air/sea)
  - Customs Clearance (for imports)
  - Arrival at Distribution Center (DC)
  - Quality & Quantity Check
  - Storage & Store Movement
  - Example: Bangladesh → Mumbai Port → DMart DC → Stores
  - Narration (for delivery):

After procurement, the next step is inbound logistics - the process of moving goods from suppliers to distribution centers and eventually to stores.
  - It starts with the supplier dispatching goods. For example, 10,000 shirts are shipped from Bangladesh. Transportation mode is decided - sea for cost efficiency in bulk shipments, or air if speed is critical but costlier. If products are imported, they must clear customs checks and duties before entry.
  - Once they arrive at the distribution center, products are unloaded, sorted, and stored. Here, retailers perform quality and quantity checks against the original order, reporting any damages or discrepancies to suppliers.
  - Finally, goods are stored at the DC and shipped to retail outlets as per demand. For example, those Bangladesh shirts might land at Mumbai port, move to DMart's Mumbai DC, and then get distributed across Maharashtra stores.
  - Inbound logistics ensures products reach the shelves on time, in good condition, and at minimal cost.

Slide 20 - Inventory Management
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Goal: right stock, right place, right time (avoid over/understock).
  - Step 1: Stock Tracking - track in/out/on-hand at DCs & stores.
  - Step 2: Bin Allocation - place SKUs in defined bins/shelves.
  - Step 3: Replenishment - reorder/transfer when levels drop.
  - Step 4: Cycle Counting - frequent small counts to keep records accurate.
  - Step 5: Balance Stock - manage stock-outs vs. excess using min/max, safety stock.
  - Example: WMS assigns 500 shirts to Bin A3-R2-S4; drops below 100 → auto alert.
  - Narration (for delivery):
"Next, let's move to the third core process--Inventory Management. The aim is simple: keep the right quantity in the right location at the right time. Too much stock locks up cash; too little stock means missed sales.
We start with Stock Tracking, monitoring what comes in, what goes out, and what remains at warehouses and stores. Then Bin Allocation puts every SKU in a defined bin or shelf so picking and replenishment are fast and accurate.
When levels fall, Replenishment rules or reorder points trigger an order or a transfer from another location. To keep records clean without halting operations, teams run Cycle Counts--small, frequent checks of selected items. Throughout, planners balance stock-outs versus overstock using tools like min/max and safety stock.
For example, a WMS might place 500 shirts in Bin A3-R2-S4. If on-hand falls below 100, the system triggers a replenishment alert so shelves don't go empty."

Slide 21 - Merchandising in Retail - Content (for PPT): - Goal: right product, price, and presentation to maximize sales. - Step 1: Assortment Planning - decide range & variety by demand/season. - Step 2: Pricing Strategy - balance value and margin (EDLP, promo, premium). - Step 3: Promotions & Offers - discounts, bundles, seasonal campaigns. - Step 4: Visual Merch & Planograms - place for visibility & easy purchase (store & online). - Example: Diwali sweets/gift hampers at entrance with Buy 1 Get 1 Free. - Narration (for delivery): "Now, let's move to Merchandising--the art and science of presenting the right products in the right way to drive conversion and profit. We begin with Assortment Planning, choosing the range and depth based on customer needs, local preferences, and seasonality. Next is Pricing Strategy, where we balance affordability and margins--using models like everyday-low-price, promotional pricing, or premium pricing. Then come Promotions & Offers--discounts, combo packs, and festival campaigns that create urgency and lift. Finally, Visual Merchandising and Planograms define where and how items appear--on endcaps, at eye level, or hero slots online--so shoppers can find and buy quickly. For instance, during Diwali, placing sweets and gift hampers at the entrance with a Buy 1 Get 1 Free offer immediately captures attention and boosts sales." Slide 22 - Sales - Content (for PPT) → - Sales: Converting customer interest into revenue. - Occurs in-store, online, or omnichannel. - Steps in Sales Process: - Customer Engagement - building trust & interest. - Product Presentation & Offers - promotions & discounts. - Point of Sale (POS) - billing & checkout. - Payment Processing - multiple secure modes. - Order Confirmation - receipt/invoice for transparency. - Example: Online smartphone purchase with discount, UPI payment & instant e-invoice. - Narration (for delivery) → "Now let's move to Sales - the stage where customer interest is finally converted into actual revenue. Sales can take place inside a physical store, online platforms, or seamlessly across an omnichannel setup. - The sales journey begins with customer engagement - where trust and interest are built, either through helpful store staff, personalized offers, or even digital recommendations. - Next comes product presentation and offers, where retailers highlight promotions, discounts, and attractive displays to push conversion. - The third step is the Point of Sale, where the checkout happens - products are scanned, discounts and loyalty points applied, and the bill is generated. - This leads to payment processing, which today is all about offering multiple secure options - cash, cards, UPI, wallets, or even Buy Now Pay Later. Smooth and secure payment is critical to a good customer experience. - Finally, order confirmation ensures transparency - the customer receives a receipt, digital invoice, or an SMS/email confirmation, which builds trust. - For example, think of a customer buying a smartphone online - they get a 10% festive discount, pay via UPI, and instantly receive an e-invoice by email. That's a complete sales flow in action." Slide 23 - Fulfillment -

receipt, digital invoice, or an SMS/email confirmation, which builds trust. - For example, think of a customer buying a smartphone online - they get a 10% festive discount, pay via UPI, and instantly receive an e-invoice by email. The retail customer journey is much more than a simple purchase--it's the entire experience a shopper has with a brand.
It starts with Awareness, when a customer first comes across a product through ads, social media, or even word-of-mouth.
Next is Consideration, where the shopper actively compares prices, reads reviews, and weighs alternatives. Retailers can influence this by offering promotions or personalized suggestions.
Then comes Purchase, where the customer finally buys--whether online or in-store. A hassle-free checkout, multiple payment methods, and smooth delivery make a huge difference here.
After the purchase, the Post-Purchase stage kicks in--covering returns, delivery, feedback, or customer service. Doing this well builds trust.
Finally, Loyalty is when the customer not only returns but also becomes an advocate for the brand. Loyalty programs and personalized rewards keep them engaged.
For example, a Zara shopper may see an ad online, browse the app, buy in-store, later return an item, and still stay loyal because of Zara's points program.

First, Category Management--here, each product category is treated like its own business unit, with separate targets for sales, profitability, and customer engagement.

Slide 28 - Retail Supply Chain Basics
  - Content (for PPT) →
  - Supply chain = backbone of retail → ensures flow of products
  - Core Flow: Supplier → DC → Warehouse/Store → Customer
  - Drop Shipping: Direct supplier → customer; low cost, slower delivery
  - Cross-Docking: Direct transfer inbound → outbound; reduces warehousing
  - Last-Mile Delivery: Costliest & most customer-visible step
  - Examples: Flipkart (warehouses + drop shipping), BigBasket (own fleet for last-mile)
  - Narration (for delivery) →

The retail supply chain is the backbone of operations. It ensures products move efficiently from suppliers to customers, balancing speed, cost, and availability.
  - First, the core flow: goods usually travel from suppliers, to distribution centers, then to warehouses or stores, and finally to the customer. This structure balances bulk handling with local availability.
  - Next, drop shipping - here, the supplier ships directly to the customer. It saves inventory costs for the retailer, though it may result in longer delivery timelines.
  - Another model is cross-docking, where goods skip warehousing altogether and move straight from inbound trucks to outbound trucks. This reduces storage costs and speeds up delivery.
  - Finally, the most expensive and customer-visible part - last-mile delivery. This is where products move from a store or hub to the customer's doorstep. Retailers invest heavily here, because delivery speed and accuracy directly shape customer satisfaction.
  - For instance, Flipkart uses warehouses for fast-moving goods but also drop ships low-demand SKUs, while BigBasket focuses on last-mile efficiency with its own delivery fleet.

Private labels are in-house brands that retailers create and sell in their own stores. Instead of relying only on national brands, retailers source or manufacture products under their own label.

Slide 33 - Loss Prevention & Shrinkage in Retail
  - Content (for PPT) →
  - Shrinkage = gap between recorded vs actual inventory
  - Causes:
  - Shoplifting/theft (customers, employees)
  - Fraud (fake returns, billing manipulation)
  - Damage/spoilage (esp. food retail)
  - Admin errors (wrong entry, miscounts)
  - Avg shrinkage = 1.5-2% of sales → major loss
  - Prevention methods:
  - CCTV & security staff
  - RFID & barcode tracking
  - Regular audits & inventory checks
  - AI & analytics → detect unusual behavior
  - Example: Walmart uses AI cameras at self-checkout to reduce theft
  - Narration (for delivery) →
Shrinkage in retail refers to the difference between what's recorded in inventory and what's actually available. This is a critical issue because retail already works on thin margins, and shrinkage averages 1.5-2% of total sales worldwide.
  - Shrinkage happens due to multiple reasons - shoplifting or theft, both by customers and sometimes employees; fraudulent activities like fake returns or billing manipulation; damage and spoilage, especially in food retail; and simple administrative errors like wrong data entry or miscounts.
  - To prevent these losses, retailers use several strategies:
  - CCTV and security staff to deter theft.
  - RFID and barcode systems to track product movement.
  - Regular audits and inventory checks to catch discrepancies early.
  - And now increasingly, AI and analytics to spot unusual patterns like suspicious returns or self-checkout theft.
  - For example, Walmart uses AI-enabled cameras at self-checkout counters to detect when customers skip scanning items - significantly reducing theft.
  - In summary, effective loss prevention not only saves costs but also protects profitability.

text:
Slide 47 - Summary of Learnings: Retail 101
  - Content (for PPT):
  - Retail connects producers, suppliers, and consumers through an end-to-end value chain.
  - Key stages: Procurement → Inventory Management → Order Fulfillment → Sales → Customer Service.
  - Core systems:
  - POS - Handles sales, payments, and loyalty programs.
  - OMS - Manages orders across online and offline channels.
  - WMS - Optimizes warehouse operations.
  - ERP - Integrates finance, operations, and supply chain.
  - CRM - Manages customer data, loyalty, and engagement.
  - KPIs track performance, inventory turnover, customer satisfaction, and shrinkage.
  - Modern retail is customer-first, tech-enabled, and performance-driven.
  - Narration (for delivery):
To summarize, retail is the bridge between producers, suppliers, and consumers, forming a complete value chain. The key stages in this chain include procurement, inventory management, order fulfillment, sales, and customer service. Core systems like POS, OMS, WMS, ERP, and CRM help manage sales, orders, warehouses, finance, and customer engagement efficiently. Retail performance is tracked using KPIs such as inventory turnover, customer satisfaction, and shrinkage. Today, modern retail focuses on being customer-first, leveraging technology, and continuously improving performance to stay competitive.
Retail Domain FAQs
A. Retail Fundamentals

Q: What is retail, and how is it different from wholesale?
A: Retail involves selling goods directly to consumers in small quantities, while wholesale refers to bulk selling to other businesses.

Q: What are the major retail formats?
A: Department stores, supermarkets, specialty stores, ecommerce platforms, factory outlets, convenience stores, and pop-ups.

Q: What is an omnichannel retail strategy?
A: An approach that offers customers a unified experience across channels -- store, website, mobile app, and customer service.

Q: What is a product lifecycle in retail?
A: The progression of a product through stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline -- affecting pricing and inventory decisions.

Q: What is seasonality in retail, and how does it affect planning?
A: Certain periods (festivals, weather changes) see sales spikes. Retailers adjust inventory, staffing, and promotions accordingly.

Q: What is a customer journey in retail?
A: It's the full experience a customer has with a brand -- from discovery and purchase to post-sale service and repeat engagement.
B. Retail Operations & Order Fulfillment

Q: What is the order lifecycle in retail?
A: Order placement → confirmation → picking → packing → shipping → delivery → possible return/refund.

Q: What are BOPIS, ROPIS, and dropshipping?
A: BOPIS: Buy Online, Pick Up In Store
ROPIS: Reserve Online, Pick Up In Store
Dropshipping: Supplier ships directly to customer, bypassing retailer's inventory.

Q: What is the role of the POS system in a retail store?
A: Processes billing, inventory updates, discounts, loyalty tracking, and real-time sales capture at the store.

Q: What is WMS (Warehouse Management System)?
A: It manages inventory flow in the warehouse -- including receiving, slotting, picking, packing, and shipping.

Q: What is reverse logistics?
A: The process of returning goods from the customer back to the retailer or supplier -- involving inspection, restocking, or refund.

Q: What does the 'last mile' in delivery mean?
A: The final leg of delivery -- from the distribution hub or store to the customer's doorstep. It's key to customer satisfaction.
C. Merchandising, Inventory & Supply Chain

Q: What is a SKU and why is it important?
A: A Stock Keeping Unit is a unique code assigned to each product variant -- vital for inventory tracking and reporting.

Q: What is assortment planning?
A: Strategic selection of products to stock in a store based on location, demand, season, and customer preferences.

Q: What is the difference between ATP, on-hand inventory, and safety stock?
A: ATP: Available to Promise -- sellable stock after accounting for committed stock
On-Hand: Physical quantity available
Safety Stock: Extra inventory held to prevent stockouts

Q: What is inventory turnover, and why does it matter?
A: It shows how often stock is sold and replenished. A higher rate indicates better inventory efficiency.

Q: What is a planogram?
A: A visual guide for how products should be placed on shelves to maximize visibility and sales consistency across stores.

Q: What is lead time in procurement?
A: The time between placing a purchase order and receiving the goods. Shorter lead times improve responsiveness.
D. Retail KPIs & Performance Metrics

Q: What are the top retail KPIs to track?
A: GMROI, Average Order Value (AOV), Conversion Rate, Sales per Sq. Ft., Forecast Accuracy, Churn Rate, and Sell-Through Rate.

Q: What is GMROI and why is it important?
A: Gross Margin Return on Investment indicates how much profit a retailer earns for every ₹1 invested in inventory.

Q: What's the difference between sell-in and sell-out?
A: Sell-In: Sale from brand to retailer
Sell-Out: Sale from retailer to end customer

Q: What is markdown optimization?
A: Data-driven strategy to reduce prices at the right time -- clearing stock while maintaining margins.

Q: How is promotion effectiveness measured?
A: Using metrics like lift in sales, redemption rates, profit margins, and customer conversion during campaigns.

Q: What is forecast accuracy, and why is it critical?
A: It measures how well projected demand matches actual sales -- ensuring the right inventory at the right time.
E. Systems, Roles & Domain Understanding

Q: What are the key systems used in retail tech stacks?
A: POS (Point of Sale), OMS (Order Management), WMS (Warehouse Management), ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), CRM (Customer Relationship Management).

Q: What roles are involved in a retail transformation project?
A: Business Analysts, Developers, Testers, Product Owners, Visual Merchandisers, Store Operations, and Inventory Planners.

Q: Why is QA critical in retail applications?
A: Even small bugs in pricing, stock display, or checkout can result in revenue loss or customer dissatisfaction.

Q: What is the role of a merchandiser in retail?
A: To decide what products to sell, when, where, and at what price -- ensuring availability and alignment with customer demand.

Q: What is the importance of system integration in retail?
A: Integration ensures data flow across POS, OMS, ERP, CRM -- preventing mismatches in pricing, stock, and orders.

Q: Why is domain knowledge important for BAs, QAs, and Devs in retail?
A: It helps them build smarter, user-centric solutions and test real-world flows accurately (e.g., returns, promotions, inventory updates).

Scenario: A customer follows up 3 times on the same issue, and each time a new ticket is created, confusing the resolution team.

Who Was Involved: Customer, CRM, Support Agent, Tech Team
Expected vs Actual: Expected: System should merge follow-ups under one ticket
Actual: Multiple tickets created
Impact: Redundant work, slow resolution, poor customer perception

Scenario 5: Customer Profile Missing Order History
Domain: Customer 360 View
Scenario: Customer calls support to ask about a past order, but the agent cannot see any past transactions.
What Went Wrong: Order history sync failed between OMS and CRM.
Who Was Involved: Customer, Support Agent, CRM, OMS, BA
Expected vs Actual: Expected: Full profile including purchase history visible to agent
Actual: CRM showed blank transaction list
Impact: Limited support capability, low first-call resolution rate
